title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 8 (Fragment 4)
text:
“I don’t care about your habits,” said Tonino. Nor did he. All he wanted to do was find some way out of this nightmare room and go home. “How shall we get out of here?”

“Through the ceiling,” Angelica said sarcastically.

Tonino looked upwards. There was that chandelier. If they could give it a pull, it might well rip a hole in the shoddy ceiling.

“Don’t be stupid,” said Angelica. “If there’s a spell out in front, there’s bound to be one up there to stop us getting out.”

Tonino feared she was right, but it was worth a try. He climbed from his chair onto the table. He thought he could reach the chandelier from there if he stood up. There was a violent creaking. Before Tonino could begin to stand up, the table swayed away sideways, as if all four of its legs were loose.

“Get down!” said Angelica.

Tonino got down. It was clear the table would fall to pieces if he stayed on it. Gloomily he pushed the crooked legs straight again. “So that’s no good,” he said.

“Unless,” said Angelica, suddenly bright and pert, “we steady it with a spell.”

Tonino transferred his miserable look from the table legs to her sharp little face. He sighed. The subject had been bound to come up. “You’ll have to do the spell,” he said. Angelica stared at him. He could feel his face heating up. “I hardly know any spells,” he said. “I—I’m slow.”

He had expected Angelica to laugh, and she did. But he thought she need not have laughed in such a mean, exultant way, nor keep saying, “Oh that’s good!” like that.

“What’s so funny?” he said. “You can laugh! I know all about you turning your father green. You’re no better than me!”

“Want to bet?” said Angelica, still laughing.

“No,” said Tonino. “Just make the spell.”

“I can’t,” said Angelica. It was Tonino’s turn to stare, and Angelica’s turn to blush. A thin bright pink spread right up the bulge of her forehead, and she put her chin up defiantly. “I’m hopeless at spells,” she said. “I’ve never got a spell right in my life.” Seeing Tonino still staring, she said, “It’s a pity you didn’t bet. I’m much worse than you are.”

Tonino could not credit it. “How?” he said. “Why? Can’t you learn spells either, then?”

“Oh, I can learn them all right.” Angelica took up the broken tap again and scribbled angrily with it, great yellow scratches in the varnished top of the table. “I know hundreds of spells,” she said, “but I always get them wrong. For a start, I’m tone-deaf. I can’t sing a tune right to save my life. Like now.” Carefully, as if she was a craftsman doing a fine carving, she peeled up a long yellow curl of varnish from the table, using the tap as a gouge. “But it’s not only that,” she said angrily, following her work intently. “I get words wrong too—everything wrong. And my spells always work, that’s the worst of it. I’ve turned all my family all colors of the rainbow. I’ve turned the baby’s bath into wine, and the wine into gravy. I turned my own head back to front once. I’m much worse than you. I daren’t do spells. About all I’m good for is under standing cats. And I even turned my cat purple too.”

Tonino watched her working away with the tap, with rather mixed feelings. If you looked at it practically, this was the worst possible news. Neither of them had a hope against the powerful spell-maker who had caught them. On the other hand, he had never met anyone who was worse at spells than he was. He thought, a little smugly, that at least he had never made a mistake in a spell, and that made him feel good. He wondered how the Casa Montana would feel if he kept turning them all colors of the rainbow. He imagined the stern Petrocchis must hate it. “Doesn’t your family mind?” he asked.

vocabulary.definition: Used to indicate the minimum case or to add a positive note.
vocabulary.example: At least he had never made a mistake in a spell.

vocabulary.definition: Certain or destined to happen.
vocabulary.example: The subject had been bound to come up.

vocabulary.definition: The upper interior surface of a room.
vocabulary.example: ‘Through the ceiling,’ Angelica said sarcastically.

vocabulary.definition: A decorative hanging light with branches for several light bulbs or candles.
vocabulary.example: Tonino looked upwards. There was that chandelier.

vocabulary.definition: Words that frequently occur together as a fixed combination.
vocabulary.example: meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations.

vocabulary.definition: A prolonged high-pitched sound caused by friction or movement.
vocabulary.example: There was a violent creaking before Tonino could begin to stand up.

vocabulary.definition: Not straight or aligned; bent.
vocabulary.example: He pushed the crooked legs straight again.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing resistance or bold disobedience.
vocabulary.example: She put her chin up defiantly.

vocabulary.definition: Triumphant or very joyful.
vocabulary.example: She need not have laughed in such a mean, exultant way.

vocabulary.definition: To make an error or mistake with something.
vocabulary.example: I always get them wrong.

vocabulary.definition: To scoop out or cut a groove in a surface with a sharp tool.
vocabulary.example: Using the tap as a gouge, she peeled up a long yellow curl of varnish from the table.

vocabulary.definition: To say repeatedly or continuously.
vocabulary.example: Nor keep saying, ‘Oh, that’s good!’ like that.

vocabulary.definition: Having both positive and negative emotions about something.
vocabulary.example: Tonino watched her working with rather mixed feelings.

vocabulary.definition: A very frightening or unpleasant dream or situation.
vocabulary.example: All he wanted to do was find some way out of this nightmare room and go home.

vocabulary.definition: Equivalent or equally bad or disadvantaged as someone or something else.
vocabulary.example: You’re no better than me!

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is sensible or realistic rather than theoretical.
vocabulary.example: If you looked at it practically, this was the worst possible news.

vocabulary.definition: To tear a hole forcibly in something.
vocabulary.example: It might well rip a hole in the shoddy ceiling.

vocabulary.definition: Of poor quality or inferior workmanship.
vocabulary.example: It might well rip a hole in the shoddy ceiling.

vocabulary.definition: In a self-satisfied or complacent manner.
vocabulary.example: He thought, a little smugly, that at least he had never made a mistake in a spell.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words with magical power; a charm.
vocabulary.example: You’ll have to do the spell, he said.

vocabulary.definition: Make or become firm, stable, or balanced.
vocabulary.example: We steady it with a spell.

vocabulary.definition: Moved slowly or rhythmically backward and forward or from side to side.
vocabulary.example: The table swayed away sideways as if all four of its legs were loose.

vocabulary.definition: Unable to distinguish different musical notes.
vocabulary.example: I’m tone-deaf. I can’t sing a tune right to save my life.

translations.ru: сделать кого-то зелёным (означает стать завидным или больным)

vocabulary.definition: To cause someone to look sick or envious.
vocabulary.example: You can laugh! I know all about you turning your father green.

vocabulary.definition: A liquid applied to a surface to give it a hard glossy finish.
vocabulary.example: She peeled up a long yellow curl of varnish from the table.
